title: Truth vs Misinformation Speed
description: A 2018 MIT study found false news spreads 6× faster than true news. Watch why — and what it means.

misinformation_appeal.label: Misinformation Appeal
misinformation_appeal.description: Emotional salience of false news (novel, outrageous, fear-inducing)

truth_appeal.label: Truth Appeal
truth_appeal.description: Emotional salience of accurate correction (boring, nuanced)

narration.text: Red = misinformation spreading. Green = truth spreading. Both start with 2 people. Same network. Different content.
Misinformation is outpacing truth. Why? It's novel, emotionally charged, and triggers the brain's threat-detection system — making it more shareable.
Truth is accurate but boring. It's nuanced. It often requires context. The brain doesn't release dopamine for 'actually, it's complicated'.
By the time truth reaches people who've already heard the false version, the false version has emotional primacy — it arrived first and felt more real.
MIT study (Vosoughi et al., 2018): false news reached 1,500 people 6× faster than true news on Twitter. And humans, not bots, were responsible for most of the spread.
Corrections rarely catch up. The optimal intervention is prebunking — warning people about manipulation tactics before they see the misinformation.
